headline: Soccer stadium proposal — new financing details surface, applying for Ohio $1B sports facility fund
paragraphs: New filings reviewed by The Blade (April 14) clarify what's actually being pitched: the $83.6M, 7,500-seat stadium is being advanced by Summit Street Sports on dormant land off Summit Street near Toledo Pickle Company, directly across the river from Glass City Metropark. The group is targeting a United Soccer League franchise and a 2028–29 opening, with capacity to host 140+ events per year (matches plus concerts). Both Summit Street Sports and the Toledo Mud Hens (for Fifth Third Field renovation) applied for a share of Ohio's new $1B sports facility fund.

The fund is now frozen. A Franklin County magistrate granted a preliminary injunction on March 9, 2026, blocking Ohio from transferring unclaimed funds to the Sports and Cultural Facilities Fund — the mechanism that would have delivered a share of the $400 million available after $600 million was earmarked for the Cleveland Browns stadium. Magistrate Jennifer Hunt found the plaintiffs demonstrated a 'substantial likelihood of success' in their argument that the transfer amounts to an unconstitutional taking under the Ohio Constitution. Summit Street Sports' $20.9 million request has no current path to disbursement while the litigation plays out.
why_it_matters: The injunction converts 'state award timing is the gating decision' to 'litigation timeline is the gating decision.' Summit Street Sports now needs either the court case to resolve in Ohio's favor, or an alternative funding stack — private capital, local TIF, a new legislative vehicle — to maintain the 2028–29 opening target. At that timeline, every quarter of litigation adds pressure on the USL franchise application and on the $241M mixed-use district that anchors the surrounding Vistula investment thesis.
